ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for the specific Singaporean economy-rice combination of white rice with one vegetable and two meat dishes. The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic cooked rice dish class (FOODON_00002678).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Rice dishes) is the closest defensible category for this mixed rice plate. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the Singaporean economy-rice format with variable vegetable and meat accompaniments.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite Singaporean rice dish combination.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economy rice with one vegetable dish and two meat dishes, as typically served at a Singaporean hawker centre or coffee shop, weighing approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: White Rice With 1 Veg & 2 Meat — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'White Rice With 1 Veg & 2 Meat' (cr_id G00010636). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB entry; the value is estimated from the total fat composition typical of a mixed meat-and-rice dish using USDA FoodData Central analogous data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled, average)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite Singaporean economy-rice plate (white rice + 1 vegetable + 2 meat). The GI of 72 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice (average GI ~72, glucose reference). The composite dish GI is dominated by the white rice base, which constitutes the majority of the carbohydrate load. Protein and fat from the two meat portions moderately reduce the effective glycemic response but do not shift the GI category from high. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 25.19 × 4.0) / 100 ≈ 72.5, rounded to 72.6 using 400 g serving and 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg per 100 g; two meat portions may substantially increase total meal sodium depending on preparation method
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request steamed or grilled meat preparations and avoid braised, salted, or soy-sauce-based dishes to keep total meal sodium within the recommended 600 mg per meal limit; the potassium content (327 mg per 100 g) partially offsets sodium load but does not eliminate risk at higher serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation; carbohydrate load 25.19 g per 100 g yields a high glycemic load per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or mixed-grain rice to lower the GI to the medium range, and choose lean steamed or grilled protein preparations to avoid saturated fat that worsens insulin resistance; portion size should be limited to reduce total glycemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from two unspecified meat portions and moderate sodium (231 mg per 100 g) present cardiovascular risk; cholesterol is reported as 0 mg per 100 g in the HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should select lean, low-saturated-fat meat preparations (steamed fish, grilled skinless chicken) and avoid fried or braised meats to limit saturated fat and sodium intake; the dietary fibre (3.7 g per 100 g) from the vegetable portion provides modest cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Two unspecified meat portions carry high probability of red meat inclusion (pork, beef, lamb), which elevates arachidonic acid intake and amplifies inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid red meat in both meat portions and instead choose fatty fish (salmon, sardines) or tofu; nightshade vegetables should be avoided in the single vegetable portion, and the reduced vegetable proportion in this combination limits anti-inflammatory fibre and antioxidant benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White Rice with 1 Vegetable and 2 Meat is a standard Singaporean economy-rice (cai fan) plate served at hawker centres and coffee shops throughout Singapore. The dish consists of a base of steamed white jasmine rice accompanied by one cooked vegetable dish and two meat or protein dishes selected from a display of pre-cooked options. Typical vegetable choices include stir-fried leafy greens (kangkong, kai lan, cabbage) or braised tofu, while meat choices range from braised pork belly, fried chicken, steamed fish, char siu (barbecued pork), and egg preparations. The rice is soft and slightly sticky with a neutral flavour, while the accompanying dishes contribute savoury, umami-rich flavours from soy sauce, oyster sauce, or sambal-based preparations. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is 3.7 g per 100 g, contributed primarily by the vegetable portion. Sodium is 231 mg per 100 g, which is borderline for hypertension management, and potassium at 327 mg per 100 g partially offsets sodium load. The white rice base carries a high glycemic index (~72), making this dish a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management without substitution to lower-GI grains. The two-meat combination increases the risk of higher saturated fat and sodium intake depending on preparation method. This dish is a culturally ubiquitous, affordable, and nutritionally variable meal that is central to the daily diet of Singaporeans across all ethnic groups.

confidence_reason: Core macro and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010636), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice as the closest defensible analogous source, with the estimation basis clearly documented. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data and documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data for a mixed rice dish with meat and vegetables, as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be updated when HPB publishes disaggregated fatty acid data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean economy-rice dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg per 100 g in the HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010636); this is plausible for a dish where the meat preparation method and cuts are unspecified and the HPB composite measurement may reflect an average across preparations. 4. The dish composition (vegetable type and meat type) is variable by stall and day; all nutrient values represent the HPB composite average. 5. Halal status is marked true as the dish is classified under Malay cuisine in the HPB source; however, individual stall compliance with halal certification should be verified. 6. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI may be modestly lower due to protein and fat from meat portions slowing gastric emptying.
